content.text: Why Handoff Quality Determines Build Quality

A prototype that validates your conversation design is only valuable if the team building the feature knows why decisions were made, what the constraints are, and what evidence backs the acceptance criteria. Without structured handoff artefacts, engineers guess at intent, QA writes tests against the wrong behaviour, and product managers can't tell if the shipped feature matches what was approved.
The handoff package transforms 'we tested it and it worked' into 'here is the evidence, here are the constraints, here are the acceptance criteria — and here is how to verify you met them.'

Good handoff establishes a traceability chain: every build ticket traces back to a prototype decision, which traces back to an eval result, which traces back to a requirement. When something breaks in production you can walk that chain in reverse to find where the assumption failed.

attrs.alt: Traceability chain: Requirement → Script Prototype → Eval Evidence → Build Ticket

content.text: Each link in the chain serves a different audience. The requirement is product language; the prototype is design language; the eval evidence is engineering language; the build ticket is project management language. The handoff package translates across all four.

A complete handoff package contains five artefact types. Each is generated from the prototype work you already did — this is not extra documentation, it is a structured export of the evidence you already have.
: the must-do / must-not-do list with test_signal for each item. This becomes the source of truth for AC.
: the HAPPY/BOUNDARY/NEGATIVE/RECOVERY scripts with outcome annotations showing which behaviour they exercise.
: pass/fail rate per must-do item, with sample inputs and outputs that illustrate both passing and failing cases.
: the top failure modes identified during testing, with severity, RPN score, and proposed mitigations.
: a spreadsheet/JSON linking REQ-NNN → PROTO-NNN → EVAL-NNN → TICKET-NNN.
Generating the Handoff Package with Claude
Use a structured tool call to generate all five artefacts in one pass from your behaviour spec and eval results.

The traceability matrix is the spine of the handoff. Generate it programmatically from your requirement IDs, prototype IDs, and eval run data so it is always current and never drifts from the actual evidence.
Writing Acceptance Criteria from Prototype Evidence

Acceptance criteria derived from prototype evidence are more precise than criteria written from requirements alone. They specify the exact behaviour observed in the prototype and set the measurement threshold from the eval.
CI Gate: Block Build Without Prototype Evidence
Add a CI check that fails the build if any AI-feature ticket is merged without a linked eval result. This keeps the traceability chain from breaking under delivery pressure.

# .github/workflows/prototype-evidence-gate.yml
name: Prototype Evidence Gate

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, labeled]

jobs:
  check-evidence:
    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'ai-feature')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Verify prototype evidence
        run: python3 scripts/check_prototype_evidence.py
        env:
          PR_BODY: ${{ github.event.pull_request.body }}
          REQUIRED_SECTIONS: |
            ## Prototype Evidence
            ## Acceptance Criteria

# scripts/check_prototype_evidence.py
import os, sys, re

pr_body = os.environ.get("PR_BODY", "")
required_sections = [
    "## Prototype Evidence",
    "## Acceptance Criteria",
]
required_refs = [
    r"EVAL-\d+",   # must reference at least one eval result
    r"PROTO-\d+",  # must reference at least one prototype
]

missing_sections = [s for s in required_sections if s not in pr_body]
missing_refs = [p for p in required_refs if not re.search(p, pr_body)]

if missing_sections or missing_refs:
    print("FAIL: PR is missing prototype evidence.")
    if missing_sections:
        print(f"  Missing sections: {missing_sections}")
    if missing_refs:
        print(f"  Missing references: {missing_refs}")
    sys.exit(1)

print("PASS: Prototype evidence verified.")
Run this checklist before handing off to engineering. Any NO means the handoff is not ready.
Every prototype script has eval evidence
All eval results have a pass/fail status
FAIL evals are marked BLOCKED in ticket tracker
Each build ticket has Given/When/Then AC
AC thresholds are drawn from eval data
Failure modes with RPN > 100 have mitigations in tickets
Traceability matrix is committed to the repo
CI gate for prototype evidence is enabled
: 'We tested it and it worked' is not evidence. Engineering needs the eval numbers, not a verbal summary.
: acceptance criteria written after implementation confirm what was built, not what was required. Write AC from prototype evidence before engineering begins.
: tickets with no prototype link force engineers to guess at intent. Link every AI-feature ticket to its PROTO-NNN and EVAL-NNN artefacts.
: a FAIL eval that proceeds to build ships a known defect. Block the ticket until the prototype is revised and the eval passes.
: a matrix written once and never updated goes stale. Generate it from source data so it regenerates automatically on each handoff.

A prototype-to-build handoff packages the evidence from your lo-fi prototype work into artefacts that engineering and QA can act on directly: a behaviour spec with test signals, annotated script prototypes, eval summaries with pass/fail status, a failure mode registry, and a traceability matrix linking requirements to tickets. Acceptance criteria derived from eval thresholds give engineering a measurable definition of done. A CI gate enforces that AI-feature tickets carry this evidence before merging, keeping the traceability chain intact under delivery pressure.
